detail: 4 of ~16 task units done (T2+T2b, T3+T4, T6+T13partial); 1203 tests green; user flagged that grammar-parser still accepts :Tag(args) pattern syntax — full canonical transition needs scope expansion before T8/T13/T14
reflection: Key finding: T6's FMPL parser rejection is necessary but not sufficient. The grammar parser at grammar/parser.rs:1309-1140 has its own :Tag(child_patterns) syntax that emits Pattern::TagMatch — independent of Expr::Tagged. Migrating grammar action bodies (test fixtures, tavern demo) was sufficient to make 1203 tests green, but the grammar PATTERN side of those fixtures is still legacy. Pattern::TagMatch has 27 references across compiler.rs/vm.rs/grammar runtime/trampoline/pattern/repr — deleting it (T14) is bigger than originally scoped. Second key finding: FMPL inline pattern blocks (cmd @ { pat => body, ... }) can't disambiguate end-of-body from start of next [...]-pattern without explicit separators. Discovered when tavern dispatch arms tried '[:Talk, n] => ...' on a line after 'desc' — parser tried to make 'desc[:Talk, ...]' postfix index. Fix was adding ';' separators in tavern.fmpl. Could improve parser look-ahead later but tabled for now.

action: ITER-0004x SCENARIO-0109 first run: 12/29 pass, 17 fail
detail: first-run failures revealed three latent cross_compile.rs bugs (hardcoded I64 ret_types, missing PushScope/PopScope/Copy opcodes, LoadVar zero-placeholder)
reflection: A parity gate's job is to surface latent bugs, not to confirm prior beliefs. SCENARIO-0109 was designed to prove dual-VM parity for the 'supported subset'; instead its first run proved the supported subset was a lie - the rustdoc audit (T1) had been wrong about what cross_compile actually handled. The right response to 17 failing cases is NOT to narrow the scenario to the passing subset; it is to investigate each failure and let the gate drive coverage to completion. The user explicitly redirected me away from the narrowing impulse: 'INtegers are easy for VMs. Its the larger primatives that are interesting.' Generalizable: verification-gate design should treat first-run failures as discoveries, not as embarrassments to paper over.

It does NOT prove execution_tape can host FMPL's value model (lists, maps, tagged shapes, closures), FMPL's control flow (if/match/&&/||/return), or FMPL's pattern matching. The user noticed the leap immediately. Generalizable: when summarizing verification evidence, the claim's scope must match the evidence's scope. detail: MigrationEngine pattern (moor-echo) became the foundational T0 task; invalidation correctly identified as wrong-fit and deferred; cairn span-discipline split off as separate iteration candidate

Before the study: ITER-0005 was 'land the envelope + payload writers, fall back to source recompilation on incompatibility.' After: ITER-0005 has a NEW foundational T0 task (MigrationEngine infrastructure) that converts schema migration from 'reject + recompile' to 'rewrite payload via composite vN→vN+1 rule chain.' Generalizable pattern: BEFORE finalizing an iteration's scope card, scan sibling projects under ~/development/ for prior art. Cost: ~30 minutes of reading. Payoff: scope cards that adopt proven patterns instead of inventing inferior ones. Recorded in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-12-lessons-from-siblings.md as a reusable methodology.

action: Codebase audit during cairn study: FMPL drops spans at the AST boundary
detail: cairn keeps Span on every lower::Instr variant + tape.rs threads via set_span(); FMPL Instruction enum has zero span fields
reflection: Architectural gap discovered while comparing cairn's lower.rs to FMPL's compiler.rs. Cairn pattern: every Instr variant has a 'span: Span' field, every emit site calls 'self.set_span(*span)' before the bytecode write, and execution_tape's SpanId surfaces spans at runtime traps. FMPL pattern: spans tracked at lexer/token boundary, dropped at the AST/Expr boundary, never re-attached. Result: runtime errors in fmpl-core cannot point back at source byte ranges. Cost to fix: one iteration of work to add Span field to every Instruction variant and wire it through ~50-80 emit sites in compiler.rs. Payoff: permanent diagnostic quality improvement. NOT scoped into ITER-0005 (orthogonal to persistence) - tracked as a separate iteration candidate in the lessons doc.

action: User stated preference: prefer proof-like tests confirming full properties of code over pointwise tests
detail: preference captured 2026-05-12 during post-ITER-0004x reflection session
reflection: User preference: when authoring tests/scenarios, prefer proof-like assertions that confirm full properties of the code over pointwise tests that only exercise a finite set of specific inputs. Concrete examples of the distinction: (a) for cross_compile.rs, a property-based test that confirms 'for every well-typed FMPL expression in the supported subset, in-tree Vm and execution_tape Vm produce equal results' is preferred over SCENARIO-0109's hand-curated 29 cases. (b) for parser-rejection scenarios, a structural assertion 'no AST node of type Expr::Tagged can be produced from any input' is preferred over a list of specific rejected source strings. (c) for opcode-rename invariants, a typed-proof that 'the variant Instruction::MakeTagged is not constructible because it does not exist as a name' is preferred over greps that check 0 matches in src/. Applies retroactively: SCENARIO-0109 should ideally grow toward a proptest-style generator that emits well-typed expressions over the supported subset; SCENARIO-0106 greps are pointwise stand-ins for typed-deletion proofs. Generalizable to: prefer proptest-style generators, prefer typed invariants (won't-compile-if-violated), prefer exhaustive case analysis (enum match without _ pattern) over input enumeration. Recorded as durable feedback memory and indexed in MEMORY.md as feedback_prefer_proof_tests.md.

action: Hegel evaluation 2026-05-12: cross-lang property-test framework with Hypothesis shrinker; deferred out of scope
detail: verdict: drop for now (Python server + beta status outweigh selling points today); revisit if property tests surface failures hard to triage with proptest-quality minimization
reflection: Dependency-evaluation framework that emerged from this session: (1) Acceptable - public Rust crates that earn compile-time cost. (2) Acceptable - tracking compile time as a real cost. (3) Not acceptable - depending on non-public projects like moor-echo or cairn (borrow patterns instead, implement in-house). (4) Wary - depending on projects that bring other language runtimes (Hegel's Python server is exactly this case). For Hegel specifically: its selling point is Hypothesis-quality shrinking of failing property-test inputs - better minimization than proptest/quickcheck. The Python-server cost is the price. Today FMPL has no property tests at all, so there is nothing to shrink and the dependency adds cost without payoff. If FMPL ever has property tests AND those tests start surfacing failures that proptest-quality shrinking can't minimize well enough for triage, the calculation could flip. Until then, deferred. Recorded so the reasoning is preserved if the question resurfaces - explicitly NOT a permanent rejection.

action: Both ITER-0005a → 0005a.{0,1,2} and ITER-0005a.2 → 0005a.{2,3} splits were driven by reader/writer concern asymmetry
detail: Recurring scope-split heuristic: writer-side and reader-side changes belong in distinct iterations
reflection: When a scope card bundles both writer-side and reader-side changes to the same surface, prefer splitting along that axis. The 2026-05-13 PAR scope review on ITER-0005a.2 flagged that AC-5 (write-side: route save_to_fjall through envelope helper) and AC-7 (read-side: surface LoaderStats public API + rewire load_from_fjall through decode) were structurally distinct concerns bundled in one iteration. Split into 0005a.2 (writer-only) + 0005a.3 (reader-only). Same pattern already appeared in the original ITER-0005a → 0005a.1 (envelope format + loader) + 0005a.2 (call-site sweep) split — that was also writer-add vs writer-rewire on the same axis. Generalizable heuristic: at scope-review time, look for 'this iteration touches both write paths and read paths of the same artifact' as a fracture line. If found, split. Each side has its own consumer story, its own test surface, and its own correctness invariants. Bundling them produces oversized iterations and silently elides one side (usually the read side, because writer changes look more concrete on a card).

detail: cmd='python3 .agent/tools/memory_reflect.py \\\n  "iteration-split-writer-reader-asymmetry" \\\n  "Both ITER-0005a → 0005a.{0,1,2' | out={'timestamp': '2026-05-13T05:57:30.894075+00:00', 'skill': 'iteration-split-writer-reader-asymmetry', 'action': 'Both ITER-0005a → 0005a.{0,1,2} and ITER-0005a.2 → 0005a.{2,3} splits were driven by re

action: Two consecutive PAR reviews (0005a.1, 0005a.2) flagged signatures referencing types from downstream iterations
detail: Reliable PAR-finding pattern: scope cards sketch helpers that depend on types from yet-to-ship iterations
reflection: PAR scope reviews reliably catch a specific pattern: a scope card sketches a helper signature that references a type/concept from a downstream iteration. Examples: ITER-0005a.0 (deferred) referenced PersistedRecord which wasn't pinned anywhere; ITER-0005a.2 (PAR-revised 2026-05-13) referenced Option<Hash> where Hash was a hypothetical type from ITER-0005b. Both iterations had the type 'shipping later' as the justification. Both PARs caught it as Critical. The boxing-in cost is real: when the unborn type finally arrives, its shape may not match what the helper was committed to, forcing either retrofit-with-breakage or the helper grows a wrapper. Disposition: either (a) replace the parameter with the concrete type that exists today (e.g., [u8; 32] sentinel instead of Option<Hash>) and add the wrapper newtype when the downstream iteration arrives, or (b) defer the helper itself until the type arrives. Both PARs picked (a). Worth saving as a PAR-finding pattern: 'check for helper signatures that reference types from future iterations; either ground them in concrete present-day types or defer the helper.'

detail: cmd='python3 .agent/tools/memory_reflect.py \\\n  "par-finding-boxing-in-via-unborn-types" \\\n  "Two consecutive PAR reviews (00' | out={'timestamp': '2026-05-13T05:57:45.126135+00:00', 'skill': 'par-finding-boxing-in-via-unborn-types', 'action': 'Two consecutive PAR reviews (0005a.1, 0005a.2) flagged signatures referencing types from

action: ITER-0005a.2 PAR catch: scope card said 'three save_to_fjall callers' but actual code had four call sites with four different shape combinations
detail: Discipline: read each affected call site's actual code before finalizing a sweep scope card, not just the imagined surface
reflection: Before finalizing a scope card that sweeps an existing surface (refactor/rewrite/route-through-new-helper), read each affected call site's actual code. The scope card for ITER-0005a.2 originally said 'three save_to_fjall callers' and 'plus raw serde patterns in stream_input.rs' — sounds like 3-4 mechanical changes. Actual code (compiler.rs:697, object.rs:177, grammar/incremental.rs:71, grammar/stream_input.rs:457,464,468,551,554) has four distinct patterns across the four files: (a) feature-gated single-record write; (b) feature-gated TWO-record-shape per save (__object_ids__ index + per-object records); (c) UNGATED single-record write; (d) ungated panic-on-write (.expect()) raw-serde with TWO distinct write sites (positions + memo). Each variant pinned a separate decision the scope card hadn't anticipated. PAR caught all four as Serious findings. Discipline: at scope-card finalization, walk each affected call site, note its shape combination (gated × cardinality × error-discipline × access-pattern), and reflect those in the build order. Not 'three callers' but 'four call sites, each with these specific shape considerations: ...'. Generalizes the sibling-project-study heuristic (which says 'read external code') to in-repo code: 'read in-repo code at every site a sweep will touch.'
